story:
The drive is only thirty minutes, but I feel every second. Usually, Ava and I blast music, windows down, roof off my Jeep, her switching from my rock to whatever Taylor Swift she wants. Today, it’s quiet. She stares out the window, blonde hair falling over her bare shoulders, sunglasses hiding hazel eyes, an oversized navy tank covering her swimsuit. I grip the wheel and wonder if bringing her is a mistake. Once we get there, I can’t take it back.

I grew up between Clearwater and Tampa, happiest near the water. My grandparents’ home sat right on the beach, and I still remember waking to coffee and salt air, the giant window in our room opening to sand less than a hundred feet away. My friends chose skate parks and arcades; I chose birthdays by the ocean. I studied there, celebrated there, healed there. The ocean made the rest of the world feel small, and I loved that.

So everyone was surprised when I majored in criminal justice at UT instead of marine science. I still don’t know why I chose it, but I’m grateful—UT is where I met Ava. We’ve been together six months, and I’m already picturing asking her to move in after graduation. But before any big step, I have to take her to the spot.

My grandfather died a month before sophomore year. My grandmother had to sell the beach house and move in with my parents. I missed everything, and my grades slid. One rough day, I called her in tears. She sent me an address halfway between us.

I pulled into a crowded restaurant perched on a long bridge between Tampa and Clearwater, the only stop for miles. She hugged me, then led me down a hidden walkway to a slip of sand beneath the restaurant, where rocks made a chair—one flat on the sand, another propped behind it. We sat and faced the open water. To our left, Tampa’s skyline; to our right, Clearwater; in front of us, nothing but blue. The waves lapped over pebbles, and faint music drifted down from above. Peace settled in.

“Your grandfather and I used to come here,” she said. It had been a different restaurant, but the cutout had always been here. She hadn’t seen anyone else find it. She told me they dreamed there, made up there, and that here, on this rock, she told him she was pregnant with my dad. She smiled and said she felt him with us.

We met at the spot every Sunday for a year—sometimes with picnics, sometimes with books, always with the ocean. She talked to him, and I talked about school, work, and my clumsy attempts at dating. Her laugh and the waves made everything easier. In her last year, she became my best friend. She died at the end of my junior fall. I tried to keep going on Sundays. It hurt. I stopped until I met Ava.

Then I started coming back, telling the water about her: our project, our first date, how different this felt. No one ever noticed me slipping down the path. It was always ours.

Now I park beside Ava, open her door, and take her hand. I guide her over sand and rock, the smack of her flip-flops too loud against the hush of waves. Doubt creeps in. At the rock chair, I wrap an arm around her.

“My grandmother and I used to come here,” I say. The ocean steadies me. The air hums with something warm.

“It’s been a while since I felt her this strongly,” I tell Ava. “I think she’s happy I brought you.”
